output: OPEC+ agreed Sunday to extend its current production cuts of 2.2 million barrels per day through the end of 2026. The decision was largely expected by markets. Saudi Arabia's energy minister said the alliance retained flexibility to adjust output.

Streaming service Vortex reported its first profitable quarter on Tuesday, posting net income of $87 million. Subscriber growth slowed to 1.2 million additions, well below the 2.4 million Wall Street expected. Shares fell 6% after-hours on the subscriber miss.

A pediatric ward fire at a public hospital in Karachi killed at least 14 children early Sunday, authorities said. An electrical short was identified as the preliminary cause. The hospital had failed two recent fire-safety inspections, according to local reports.

Australia's competition regulator approved Telstra's $1.2 billion acquisition of regional ISP Skyline Networks, with conditions requiring divestiture of overlapping fiber assets in two states. Telstra said the conditions were 'workable' and expects to close the transaction by year-end. Industry analysts said the precedent could shape future telecom consolidation.

Scientists at the Large Hadron Collider announced preliminary evidence of an unexpectedly heavy variant of the W boson, potentially in tension with the Standard Model. The result, presented at a Geneva conference, carries a statistical significance of 3.4 sigma — below the 5-sigma threshold for discovery. Theorists are already proposing extensions to accommodate the result.

A magnitude 5.8 earthquake struck off the coast of northern Chile early Tuesday, the U.S. Geological Survey reported. Power was knocked out in three coastal towns and at least 12 buildings sustained structural damage. Local officials said no fatalities had been reported as of midday.

Researchers at MIT reported in Nature on Thursday that a new battery chemistry using sodium-ion electrodes achieved 92% capacity retention after 5,000 charge cycles. The team said the design avoids both lithium and cobalt, easing supply-chain concerns. Commercialization remains several years off, the lead author cautioned, citing manufacturing challenges.

A coalition of 14 state attorneys general filed an antitrust lawsuit against grocery delivery company Mercado, alleging it pressured retailers to set higher in-store prices to subsidize its delivery margins. Mercado denied the claims, calling the suit 'politically motivated.' The complaint seeks structural remedies including potential divestiture.

South Korea's central bank reduced its key interest rate by 25 basis points to 3.00% due to declining export demand, with the decision supported by all board members. Additionally, the bank lowered its GDP growth projection for 2026 from 2.2% to 1.9%. Following the announcement, the won depreciated by about 0.4% against the dollar.

Vortex streaming service announced its first profitable quarter with a net income of $87 million, although subscriber growth was lower than anticipated, adding only 1.2 million instead of the expected 2.4 million. The company credited its profitability to a recent price increase and the introduction of an ad-supported tier. Following the news of the subscriber shortfall, shares dropped by 6% in after-hours trading.

The exhibition runs through January. A new exhibition at the Tate Modern, opening Friday, brings together 80 works by South Korean artist Lee Bul spanning four decades. Curator Achim Borchardt-Hume called Lee 'one of the most important sculptors working today.'

Boeing reached a tentative agreement with its largest machinist union late Thursday, potentially ending a six-week strike. The deal includes a 38% wage increase over four years and a one-time $7,000 ratification bonus. Boeing has lost an estimated $5 billion in revenue during the work stoppage.

A recent study in The Lancet revealed that adults who walk 8,000 steps for one or two days each week experience a 14.9% reduction in overall mortality over ten years. The research involved 3,100 participants from a U.S. health survey, but the authors noted that the observational nature of the study prevents establishing a direct cause-and-effect relationship. The results may help streamline physical activity recommendations.

Wildfires in southern Greece have consumed over 28,000 hectares since they started last weekend, resulting in the deaths of two firefighters and one civilian. The country has sought aerial firefighting assistance from France, Italy, and Spain through the EU's civil protection system. Strong winds are anticipated to hinder efforts to control the fires throughout the weekend.

South Korea's central bank cut its benchmark rate by 25 basis points to 3.00% on Thursday, citing weakening export demand. The decision was unanimous among the seven-member board. The bank also revised its 2026 GDP growth forecast down to 1.9% from 2.2%.

Indonesia's parliament passed a controversial new criminal code on Tuesday that prohibits cohabitation between unmarried couples and bans insults to the president. Rights groups condemned the law, which takes effect in 2026. The law also restricts certain political demonstrations.

Apple is reportedly in the final stages of negotiating the purchase of AI startup Perlin Labs for about $1.8 billion, which would be its biggest acquisition in the AI sector. Perlin, established in 2022, focuses on on-device language models. Neither Apple nor Perlin has provided comments on the situation.

Australia's competition regulator has given the green light to Telstra's $1.2 billion purchase of Skyline Networks, contingent on the sale of certain overlapping fiber assets in two states. After an 11-month review, Telstra considers the conditions manageable and aims to finalize the deal by the end of the year. Analysts believe this decision may influence future mergers in the telecommunications sector.

The exhibition runs through January. A new exhibition at the Tate Modern, opening Friday, brings together 80 works by South Korean artist Lee Bul spanning four decades. Curator Achim Borchardt-Hume called Lee 'one of the most important sculptors working today.'

Lumen Robotics introduced its first humanoid robot, Pax-1, designed for warehouse use, at a San Jose event. Priced at $48,000, it aims to serve logistics clients. The company has secured pilot agreements with two unnamed Fortune-500 retailers. This launch follows a similar product release by competitor Figure AI last quarter.
